children.name: Melanocharis
children.description: The typical berrypeckers — a genus of small, fruit-eating passerines endemic to New Guinea, with short tails and fine, slightly curved bills. Their plumage is sexually dimorphic: males are glossy blue-black or olive above, yellow or white below, while females are duller. They inhabit montane and submontane forests, where they feed primarily on small fruits and berries.

children.name: Melanocharis nigra
children.description: A small berrypecker with the male glossy blue-black above and white below, widespread across the lowlands and hills of New Guinea and nearby islands. It is the most common and widespread melanocharitid, found from sea level to 1,800 metres in forest edge and secondary growth. It feeds on small fruits and occasionally insects.

children.name: Melanocharis versteri
children.description: A montane berrypecker with a distinctive white-tipped, fan-shaped tail and the male a striking grey-black with a yellow belly, found in New Guinea from 1,400 to 3,000 metres. It forages actively in the lower canopy and understorey, taking small fruits and berries. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Rhamphocharis
children.description: A small genus of berrypeckers with notably thick, deep bills adapted for crushing seeds and hard fruits, endemic to the mountain forests of New Guinea. They are medium-sized by melanocharitid standards, with spotted underparts that provide good camouflage in the forest gloom. They remain poorly studied due to their remote montane habitat.

children.name: Rhamphocharis crassirostris
children.description: A sturdy, thick-billed berrypecker with olive-green upperparts heavily spotted with white and a whitish belly, found in montane forests of New Guinea from 1,200 to 3,300 metres. Its stout bill allows it to feed on hard seeds and fruits that are unavailable to other berrypeckers. It moves slowly and deliberately through the forest, often in small flocks.
